summary: My father came from Romania, and he was a real mathematical genius. He invented various pieces of machinery and even designed the sight used on carbine rifles in World War I. Although I grew up in New York, my father's Romanian origins and brilliance influenced my upbringing and family life.

My father designed the sight that went on all the carbine rifles used in World War I. This was a significant invention that showed his brilliance as a manufacturer and inventor. Although I was a child during that time, his work during the war was a point of pride in our family.

I attended the Ethical Culture Schools in New York, which had a tremendous influence on me. The curriculum was very thorough, covering prehistoric man in first grade through the Renaissance by sixth grade. After graduating from the Fieldston School, the high school division, I was well-prepared academically and it shaped my lifelong learning.
cues: curriculum from prehistoric man to Renaissance

summary: During World War II, my father designed the gun sight used in the conflict and helped many Jews escape from Germany. He recognized Hitler's threat early on and was actively involved in philanthropy to assist those in danger. The war years deeply affected my family and our sense of responsibility.

My father brought many Jews over from Germany because he understood the danger they faced under Hitler. He even had a lawyer and a doctor working in his factory to help with these efforts. Germany was a place of great concern for us during the rise of Nazism and World War II.

My father recognized early on what a sonofabitch Hitler was and took action to help Jews escape from Germany. We were very aware of the threat he posed during the 1930s and 1940s. His rise influenced my father's philanthropy and our family's involvement in aiding refugees.

When I was a little girl, we lived across the street from the Museum of Natural History in New York. My friends and I used to play jump rope across the street, and it was part of my childhood environment. It was near where I learned early lessons about generosity, like when I gave one of my coats to a child who needed it.
cues: childhood home near Museum of Natural History

summary: My father was a great philanthropist who gave generously to help Jews get out of Germany and supported many charities. He believed in helping his kinfolk while giving away as much money as possible. His philanthropic spirit influenced my values and actions throughout my life.

I graduated from the Fieldston School, the high school division of the Ethical Culture Schools. It was a very tough and thorough academic environment that prepared me well for college. The education I received there was foundational to my later academic and personal development.

I was accepted at Radcliffe College among other schools, but I chose to attend Antioch College instead. Radcliffe was one of the prestigious options available to me after high school. Although I didn't attend, being accepted there reflected my strong academic background.

Like Radcliffe, I was also accepted at Bryn Mawr College but ultimately decided to go to Antioch College. Bryn Mawr was another respected institution that recognized my academic abilities. These acceptances showed the range of options I had after graduating from Fieldston.

I chose to attend Antioch College despite its reputation as a home of free love and communism in those days. When I arrived, I found their academics were behind what I was used to from Fieldston. Nevertheless, Antioch influenced me a lot, especially socially and politically during my college years.
cues: reputation for free love and communism

summary: After Antioch, I did a year of graduate work at the Catholic University of America, which was an interesting experience for a Jewish girl like me. Teaching at a Catholic school later also connected back to this period. It was a unique intersection of my background and education.

My son Dan and I spent a year in Europe when he was about 12 years old, around 1961. We traveled to England and then to Copenhagen, Denmark, where we had a wonderful time living with a woman who had been part of the Danish resistance during World War II. This trip was a significant experience for both of us.

I lived in England for a time when my husband was studying psychology at the Sigmund Freud Institute in London. Earlier, Dan and I had spent a brief period in England during our European year. England was an important place in my life both during that trip and later when I lived there with my husband.

During our year in Europe, Dan and I spent most of our time in Copenhagen, Denmark. We lived with a woman who had been part of the Danish underground during World War II and who was very supportive of Jewish people. It was a warm and meaningful experience that connected us to the history of resistance against the Germans.

While living in Copenhagen, we stayed with a woman who had been part of the Danish resistance movement during World War II. She was very much in favor of Jewish people and opposed the German occupation. Her courage and values left a strong impression on me and my family.

My husband was a psychologist who studied for a year at the Sigmund Freud Institute in London. During that time, I was working with Planned Parenthood in England. The institute was part of our life in England and connected to my husband's professional development.

While living in England, I worked at Planned Parenthood, supporting their mission. I still support Planned Parenthood today. My involvement there was part of my broader commitment to social causes and health education.

I lived in Berkeley, California for about 50 years, starting when I was around 35 years old. Berkeley was a very exciting place politically, and I was deeply involved in supporting left-wing candidates and local politics. I also taught there and had memorable experiences helping students overcome challenges.

When I taught at a Catholic school, the nuns would say the 'Hail Mary' prayer before class. I would silently say the Jewish prayer 'Shema Yisrael' at the same time. It was a small but meaningful way I maintained my identity in a different religious environment.

While teaching at a Catholic school, I would recite the 'Shema Yisrael' prayer silently when the nuns said the 'Hail Mary.' The nuns once noticed and laughed when they found out what I was saying. It was a way I stayed connected to my Jewish faith in a predominantly Catholic setting.

I met Eleanor Roosevelt once while volunteering at a camp for kids from the Lower West Side of Manhattan. I was whitewashing a bathroom and dressed in painter's overalls when she was introduced to me, which was quite embarrassing. Later, she greeted me warmly at a meeting, and I learned she remembered me for what I was doing, not how I looked.

I volunteered at a camp that helped children from the Lower West Side in Manhattan, many of whom had never seen a tree before. It was rewarding work, and it was during this time that I met Eleanor Roosevelt. This experience deepened my commitment to social causes and helping underprivileged children.

I attended a meeting where John Lovejoy Elliott spoke; he was from the Elliott-Lovejoy families known for their antislavery activism. At that meeting, Eleanor Roosevelt also appeared and greeted me warmly. The connection to these historic families and leaders was inspiring and part of my social activism.

The Elliott family, along with the Lovejoys, were known for their involvement in the antislavery movement. I encountered members of this family at meetings where social justice was discussed. Their legacy was an important part of the history I connected with during my activism.

The Lovejoy family, together with the Elliott family, played a significant role in the antislavery movement. I met people from this family at events focused on social causes. Their commitment to justice resonated with my own values and activism.

I once helped a student who was struggling with reading by giving him a book about slave rebellions before the American Civil War. This book inspired him and helped him learn to read despite earlier difficulties. The history of the Civil War and its struggles for freedom became a tool for education and empowerment in my teaching.
